 {"id":"ntm-0vyj","title":"Implement rotation history and audit log","description":"## Overview
Track all rotation events for debugging and analysis.

## Location
Create `internal/context/history.go`

## Rotation Record
```go
type RotationRecord struct {
    ID              string    `json:"id"`
    Timestamp       time.Time `json:"timestamp"`
    SessionName     string    `json:"session_name"`
    AgentID         string    `json:"agent_id"`
    Profile         string    `json:"profile,omitempty"`
    
    // Context state before rotation
    ContextBefore   float64   `json:"context_before_percent"`
    EstimationMethod string   `json:"estimation_method"`
    
    // Compaction attempt
    CompactionTried bool      `json:"compaction_tried"`
    CompactionResult string   `json:"compaction_result"`
    
    // Rotation result
    Success         bool      `json:"success"`
    FailureReason   string    `json:"failure_reason,omitempty"`
    SummaryTokens   int       `json:"summary_tokens"`
    
    // Duration
    DurationMs      int64     `json:"duration_ms"`
}
```

## Storage
```
~/.local/share/ntm/rotation_history/
  <session-name>.jsonl  # Append-only log
```

## CLI Commands
```bash
ntm rotation history [session]     # List rotation events
ntm rotation history --last=10     # Last 10 rotations
ntm rotation history --failed      # Only failed rotations
ntm rotation stats                 # Rotation statistics
```

## Statistics
- Rotations per session
- Average context at rotation
- Success/failure rate
- Average rotation duration
- Most commonly rotated profiles

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] RotationRecord struct
- [ ] JSONL file storage
- [ ] ntm rotation history command
- [ ] ntm rotation stats command
- [ ] Integration with rotation flow
- [ ] Cleanup of old history","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-30T20:43:16.930328537-06:00","updated_at":"2026-01-01T20:26:42.365946883-06:00","closed_at":"2026-01-01T20:26:42.365946883-06:00","close_reason":"Implemented rotation history storage, CLI commands (history/stats/clear), and tests","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-0vyj","depends_on_id":"ntm-jv8p","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-30T20:43:26.856846968-06:00","created_by":"daemon","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"ntm-0vyj","depends_on_id":"ntm-cj0w","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-30T20:43:26.914480282-06:00","created_by":"daemon","metadata":"{}"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-20n","title":"Implement --robot-assign for work distribution recommendations","description":"AI-assisted work assignment suggestions based on current state.

## Rationale
With N agents and M ready beads, optimal assignment is non-trivial. This flag analyzes current state and recommends which agent should work on what, considering:
- Agent types and their strengths
- Current workload balance  
- Bead dependencies and priorities
- Recent activity patterns

## Usage
```bash
ntm --robot-assign myproject
ntm --robot-assign myproject --beads=ntm-abc,ntm-def  # specific beads
ntm --robot-assign myproject --strategy=balanced      # or: speed, quality
```

## Output Structure
```json
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "strategy": "balanced",
  "recommendations": [
    {
      "agent": "0.1",
      "agent_type": "claude",
      "assign_bead": "ntm-1rz",
      "confidence": 0.85,
      "reasoning": "Profiler task matches claude's code analysis strengths"
    },
    {
      "agent": "0.2", 
      "agent_type": "codex",
      "assign_bead": "ntm-7mg",
      "confidence": 0.72,
      "reasoning": "UI/styling task, codex handles CSS well"
    }
  ],
  "blocked_beads": [
    {"id": "ntm-xyz", "blocked_by": ["ntm-abc"]}
  ],
  "idle_agents": ["0.3"],
  "unassignable_beads": [
    {"id": "ntm-qqq", "reason": "Requires human input"}
  ]
}
```

## Assignment Strategies
- balanced: distribute work evenly across agents
- speed: assign to fastest/most active agents  
- quality: match task types to agent strengths
- dependency: prioritize unblocking work

## Integration with Beads
Requires parsing bd ready and bd show output to understand task graph. Consider caching bead state to avoid repeated queries.

## Future Enhancement
Could integrate with agent-mail to check agent's stated preferences or current focus.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"Gemini","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:51:50.71632-05:00","updated_at":"2026-01-03T12:21:24.943748-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-30T18:39:56.329873881-06:00","close_reason":"Implemented --robot-assign with strategies (balanced, speed, quality, dependency), agent-task matching, and structured JSON output.  {"id":"ntm-22f","title":"Implement pre/post command hooks","description":"Run user scripts before and after NTM commands.

## What This Task Does

Enable users to define hooks that run before or after specific
NTM commands. Hooks can log, notify, validate, or transform.

## Configuration

# ~/.config/ntm/config.toml

[hooks]
# Run before any send command
pre_send = "~/.config/ntm/hooks/log-prompt.sh"

# Run after spawn completes
post_spawn = "notify-send 'NTM' 'Session created'"

# Run before dangerous commands
pre_kill = "~/.config/ntm/hooks/confirm-kill.sh"

## Hook Interface

Hooks receive command context as JSON on stdin:
{
  "command": "send",
  "args": ["myproject", "--all", "hello"],
  "session": "myproject",
  "flags": {"all": true},
  "timestamp": "2025-12-10T10:00:00Z"
}

Post hooks also receive result:
{
  "command": "spawn",
  "result": "success",
  "session": "myproject",
  "panes_created": 4,
  "duration_ms": 1234
}

## Hook Execution

Pre hooks:
- Run synchronously before command
- Non-zero exit blocks the command
- Stdout captured but not displayed (log only)
- Stderr shown to user

Post hooks:
- Run asynchronously after command
- Exit code doesn't affect NTM
- Good for notifications, logging

## Disabling Hooks

For scripting or sensitive operations:
$ ntm send --no-hooks myproject 'message'

## Example Hooks

Log all prompts:
#\!/bin/bash
jq -c '{time: .timestamp, session: .session, prompt: .args[2]}' >> ~/.ntm-prompts.jsonl

Confirm before kill:
#\!/bin/bash
session=$(jq -r '.session' -)
read -p "Really kill $session? [y/N] " confirm
[[ "$confirm" == "y" ]] || exit 1

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Hooks defined in config
- [ ] Pre hooks run before command
- [ ] Pre hook failure blocks command
- [ ] Post hooks run after command
- [ ] Command context passed as JSON
- [ ] --no-hooks flag disables hooks

 {"id":"ntm-239","title":"Implement ntm health command for agent status checking","description":"Create a command to check health status of all agents in a session.

## What This Task Does

Create 'ntm health' command that checks and reports agent health:
- Process status (running/exited)
- Activity level (active/idle/stale)
- Error detection (rate limits, crashes)
- Output summary with visual indicators

## Command Interface

$ ntm health myproject
Session: myproject
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Pane │ Agent   │ Status  │ Activity │ Issues  │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 0    │ cc_1    │ ✓ OK    │ active   │ -       │
│ 1    │ cc_2    │ ✓ OK    │ idle     │ -       │
│ 2    │ cod_1   │ ⚠ WARN  │ stale    │ no output 5m │
│ 3    │ gmi_1   │ ✗ ERROR │ crashed  │ exit 1  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Overall: 2 healthy, 1 warning, 1 error

$ ntm health myproject --json
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "checked_at": "2025-12-10T10:00:00Z",
  "agents": [...],
  "summary": {"healthy": 2, "warning": 1, "error": 1}
}

## Health Detection Logic

Process status:
- Check if pane has running process (tmux list-panes -F '#{pane_pid}')
- Check process state via /proc or ps

Activity detection:
- Capture pane content, compare over time
- 'active': Content changed recently (< 30s)
- 'idle': No change, but process running (prompt visible)
- 'stale': No change for extended period (> 5m)

Error detection (regex patterns):
- Rate limit: 'rate limit', '429', 'too many requests'
- Auth error: 'authentication', 'unauthorized', '401'
- Crash: 'error', 'exception', 'panic', exit codes

## Implementation

1. Create internal/health package
2. Implement process status checker
3. Implement activity detector
4. Implement error pattern matcher
5. Create ntm health command with TUI output
6. Add --json support (depends on ntm-ws2)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ntm health shows all agents with status
- [ ] Process crashes are detected
- [ ] Rate limit patterns are detected
- [ ] Activity level shown (active/idle/stale)
- [ ] Exit code reflects overall health
- [ ] --json output supported

 {"id":"ntm-2mz5","title":"Feature: BV Robot-Markdown Token Savings","description":"# BV Robot-Markdown Token Savings

## The Problem
Smaller-context agents (Codex, Gemini) waste tokens on verbose JSON output.

## The Solution
bv provides -robot-markdown output that's 50% smaller:
```bash
bv -robot-markdown --md-compact
```

## Implementation (internal/bv/markdown.go)

### Markdown Output
```go
func GetTriageMarkdown(ctx context.Context, compact bool) (string, error) {
    args := []string{"-robot-markdown"}
    if compact {
        args = append(args, "--md-compact")
    }
    cmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, "bv", args...)
    return string(out), nil
}
```

### Format Selection by Agent Type
```go
func getAgentContext(agentType string) (string, error) {
    if agentType == "claude" {
        // Claude has large context - use JSON
        triage, _ := GetTriage(ctx)
        return json.Marshal(triage)
    }
    // Codex/Gemini - use markdown (50% savings)
    return GetTriageMarkdown(ctx, true)
}
```

### Token Budget Awareness
```go
budgets := map[string]int{
    "claude": 180000,
    "codex":  120000,
    "gemini": 100000,
}
```

## Usage
```bash
ntm --robot-triage --format=markdown
ntm work triage --format=json  # Force JSON
```

## Why This is Phase 4 (Not Earlier)

- Codex and Gemini can be added incrementally

### Claude Code
- Login: `/login` command
- Exit: `/exit` or Ctrl+C
- Auth patterns: "successfully logged in", "authenticated as"
- Continuation: "continue. Use ultrathink"

### Codex CLI
- Login: `/logout` then restart (no in-session re-auth!)
- Exit: `/exit` or Ctrl+C
- Auth: Happens on startup
- **Key difference**: Codex ALWAYS uses restart strategy

### Gemini CLI
- Login: `/auth` command
- Exit: `/exit` or Ctrl+C
- Auth patterns: TBD (need to research)
- Continuation: TBD

## The Opportunity
s2p (source-to-prompt) converts source code to LLM-ready prompts with real-time token counting. This prevents context overflow.

## Token Budgets
```go

    "claude": 180000,
    "codex":  120000,
    "gemini": 100000,

## Budget Allocation

## Implementation (internal/context/s2p.go)

### Context Preparation
```go
type S2PConfig struct {
    Files       []string
    TokenBudget int
    IncludeTree bool
    Format      string  // "xml", "markdown"
}

func prepareContext(config S2PConfig) (*S2POutput, error) {
    cmd := exec.Command("s2p",
        "--files", strings.Join(config.Files, ","),
        "--budget", fmt.Sprintf("%d", config.TokenBudget),
        "--format", config.Format,
    )
    // ...
}
```

### Agent-Specific Budgets
```go
func prepareAgentContext(files []string, agentType string) (*S2POutput, error) {
    budgets := map[string]int{
        "claude": 180000,
        "codex":  120000,
        "gemini": 100000,
    }
    return prepareContext(S2PConfig{
        Files:       files,
        TokenBudget: budgets[agentType],
        IncludeTree: true,
        Format:      "xml",
    })
}
```

    // Progressively remove elements until under budget
}
```

 {"id":"ntm-9f8k","title":"Implement restart strategy (terminate, browser switch, restart agent)","description":"# Restart Strategy Implementation

Strategy B: Quit session, switch browser account, start fresh.

## Why This is Often Better
- Near compaction: context is getting long anyway
- After compaction: minimal context to preserve
- New sessions auto-use the active browser account!

## Flow
```go
func (o *Orchestrator) executeRestartStrategy(paneID string, provider string, targetAccount *accounts.AccountState) error {
    flow := o.authFlows[provider]
    
    // 1. Terminate existing session gracefully
    log.Printf("Terminating session in pane %s", paneID)
    if err := flow.TerminateSession(paneID); err != nil {
        // Fallback: force kill
        tmux.SendInterrupt(paneID)
        time.Sleep(time.Second)
        tmux.SendInterrupt(paneID)
        time.Sleep(time.Second)
        tmux.SendKeys(paneID, "exit", true)
    }
    
    // 2. Wait for shell prompt
    if err := o.waitForShellPrompt(paneID, 10*time.Second); err != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("session did not terminate: %w", err)
    }
    
    // 3. Prompt user for browser auth
    o.promptBrowserAuth(targetAccount)
    
    // 4. Wait for user confirmation
    fmt.Println("Press ENTER when you have logged into", targetAccount.Email)
    bufio.NewReader(os.Stdin).ReadLine()
    
    // 5. Start new agent session
    projectDir := o.getProjectDir(paneID)
    agentCmd := o.cfg.GetAgentCommand(provider)
    fullCmd := fmt.Sprintf("cd %q && %s", projectDir, agentCmd)
    
    return tmux.SendKeys(paneID, fullCmd, true)
}
```

## Shell Prompt Detection
```go
func (o *Orchestrator) waitForShellPrompt(paneID string, timeout time.Duration) error {
    ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), timeout)
    defer cancel()
    
    promptPatterns := []string{
        `\$\s*$`,   // bash prompt
        `%\s*$`,    // zsh prompt
        `>\s*$`,    // generic prompt
    }
    
    ticker := time.NewTicker(500 * time.Millisecond)
    defer ticker.Stop()
    
    for {
        select {
        case <-ctx.Done():
            return ctx.Err()
        case <-ticker.C:
            output, _ := tmux.CapturePaneOutput(paneID, 5)
            for _, pattern := range promptPatterns {
                if regexp.MustCompile(pattern).MatchString(output) {
                    return nil
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T15:19:08.744774-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T15:39:04.887518-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T15:39:04.887518-05:00"}

 {"id":"ntm-a8q","title":"Implement --robot-ack for send confirmation","description":"Track and confirm message receipt by agents.

## Rationale
After --robot-send, I need to know:
1. Did the agent receive the message?
2. Did the agent acknowledge understanding?
3. Is the agent actively working on it?

Without this, I'm blind after sending - I have to poll tail output and parse for confirmation patterns.

## Proposal: Ack Tracking
When sending with --robot-send --track, ntm:
1. Watches pane output for acknowledgment patterns
2. Returns structured confirmation

## Output Structure
```json
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "sent_at": "ISO8601",
  "confirmations": [
    {
      "pane": "0.1",
      "ack_type": "prompt_returned",  // agent showed ready prompt
      "ack_at": "ISO8601",
      "latency_ms": 234
    },
    {
      "pane": "0.2",
      "ack_type": "echo_detected",    // agent echoed input
      "ack_at": "ISO8601",
      "latency_ms": 156
    }
  ],
  "pending": ["0.3"],  // still waiting
  "failed": []
}
```

## Ack Detection Patterns
- prompt_returned: Agent shows ready prompt after processing
- echo_detected: Input echoed in pane (basic confirmation)
- explicit_ack: Agent responds with understood/acknowledged
- output_started: Agent begins producing output

## Timeout Handling
--timeout=30s - wait up to 30s for acks

```go
type RoutingContext struct {
    Task        string   // task description
    TaskTags    []string // extracted task tags (from analysis)
    Preferences RoutePreferences
}

func (r *Router) FindBestAgent(ctx RoutingContext) (*Agent, float64) {
    candidates := r.GetAvailableAgents()
    
    for _, agent := range candidates {
        score := r.baseScore(agent, ctx)
        
        // Boost score for profile tag matches
        if agent.Profile != nil {
            tagMatch := computeTagOverlap(ctx.TaskTags, agent.Profile.Tags)
            score += tagMatch * r.config.ProfileBoostWeight
        }
        
        // Focus pattern matching (if task mentions files)
        if agent.Profile != nil && len(agent.Profile.FocusPatterns) > 0 {
            fileMatch := computePatternMatch(ctx.MentionedFiles, agent.Profile.FocusPatterns)
            score += fileMatch * r.config.PatternBoostWeight
        }
    }
    
    return bestCandidate, bestScore
}
```

 {"id":"ntm-bh5","title":"EPIC: Session Persistence & Recovery","description":"Sessions survive reboots and can be recreated from saved state.

## Problem Statement
Tmux sessions are ephemeral - a reboot or accidental kill loses everything:
- Agent counts, types, and arrangements must be manually recreated
- Working directory and project context is lost
- No way to 'bookmark' a useful session configuration
- Users avoid rebooting to preserve valuable sessions

## Goals
1. Capture complete session state (agents, layout, cwd, env)
2. Enable session recreation from saved state
3. Support periodic auto-snapshots for safety
4. Make session configs portable across machines

## Success Criteria
- `ntm session save` captures full session state to disk
- `ntm session restore` recreates session exactly
- Auto-snapshot on session creation (optional)
- `ntm session list --saved` shows available saved sessions

## Technical Approach
- Session state stored as JSON in ~/.local/share/ntm/sessions/
- Capture: session name, cwd, agent types/counts, layout, pane titles
- Restore: create session, spawn agents, apply layout
- Handle conflicts (session already exists) gracefully

## State Schema
```json
{
  "name": "myproject",
  "cwd": "/path/to/project",
  "agents": {"cc": 2, "cod": 1, "gmi": 1},
  "layout": "tiled",
  "panes": [{"title": "myproject__cc_1", "command": "claude"}],
  "created": "2025-12-10T10:00:00Z",
  "saved": "2025-12-10T12:00:00Z"
}
```

## User Value
- Fearless reboots - sessions are recoverable
- Consistent project setups across machines
- Reduced friction starting work each day
- Backup for accidental session kills","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:37:01.748915-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T17:01:14.543035-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T17:01:14.543035-05:00"}

type SessionState struct {

    WorkDir   string    `json:"cwd"`
    GitBranch string    `json:"git_branch,omitempty"`
    GitRemote string    `json:"git_remote,omitempty"`
    
    // Agent Configuration
    Agents    AgentSpec `json:"agents"`  // {cc: 2, cod: 1, gmi: 1}
    
    // Pane Details (for exact recreation)
    Panes     []PaneState `json:"panes"`

    Layout    string    `json:"layout"`  // "tiled", "even-horizontal", etc.
    
    // Metadata
    CreatedAt time.Time `json:"created_at"`  // Original session creation
    Version   int       `json:"version"`     // Schema version for migrations
}

type PaneState struct {
    Title     string `json:"title"`      // e.g., "myproject__cc_1"
    Index     int    `json:"index"`
    AgentType string `json:"agent_type"` // "cc", "cod", "gmi", "user"
    Command   string `json:"command"`    // The agent launch command
    Active    bool   `json:"active"`     // Was this the active pane?
}
```

## Capture Process
1. Get session info from tmux
2. Get all panes with metadata
3. Detect working directory (from pane 0 or explicit)
4. Get git context if in repo
5. Serialize to JSON
6. Write to storage location

## Command Interface
```bash
ntm session save                 # Save current session (in tmux)
ntm session save myproject       # Save specific session
ntm session save --name=alias    # Save with custom name
ntm session save --overwrite     # Overwrite existing save
```

## Storage
Location: ~/.local/share/ntm/sessions/<name>.json

Directory structure:
```
~/.local/share/ntm/sessions/
├── myproject.json
├── frontend.json
└── backend.json
```

## Implementation
```go
func CaptureSessionState(sessionName string) (*SessionState, error) {
    session, err := tmux.GetSession(sessionName)
    if err \!= nil {
        return nil, err
    }
    
    panes, err := tmux.GetPanes(sessionName)
    if err \!= nil {
        return nil, err
    }
    
    // Extract agent configuration from pane names
    agents := countAgents(panes)
    
    // Get working directory
    cwd := detectWorkDir(panes)
    
    // Get git info if applicable
    gitBranch, gitRemote := getGitInfo(cwd)
    
    return &SessionState{
        Name:      sessionName,
        SavedAt:   time.Now(),
        WorkDir:   cwd,
        GitBranch: gitBranch,
        GitRemote: gitRemote,
        Agents:    agents,
        Panes:     mapPaneStates(panes),
        Layout:    session.Layout,
        CreatedAt: session.Created,
        Version:   1,
    }, nil
}
```

 {"id":"ntm-ceb","title":"Create internal/agentmail HTTP client package","description":"# Create internal/agentmail HTTP Client Package

## Purpose

Foundation for all Agent Mail integration. This package provides a clean Go interface to the MCP Agent Mail HTTP API.

## Technical Details

### Package Structure
```
internal/agentmail/
├── client.go       # HTTP client with connection pooling, retries
├── types.go        # Go structs matching Agent Mail data model
├── tools.go        # Methods for each MCP tool (send_message, fetch_inbox, etc.)
├── resources.go    # Methods for MCP resources (resource://inbox, etc.)
├── errors.go       # Error types with Agent Mail error codes
└── client_test.go  # Unit tests with mock server
```

### Client Configuration
```go
type Client struct {
    baseURL     string       // Default: http://127.0.0.1:8765/mcp/
    bearerToken string       // From AGENT_MAIL_TOKEN or config
    httpClient  *http.Client // With timeouts, connection pooling
    projectKey  string       // Cached project path
}

func NewClient(opts ...Option) (*Client, error)
func (c *Client) IsAvailable() bool  // Health check
```

### Key Types (matching Agent Mail schema)
```go
type Agent struct {
    ID              int       `json:"id"`
    Name            string    `json:"name"`            // e.g., "GreenCastle"
    Program         string    `json:"program"`         // e.g., "claude-code"
    Model           string    `json:"model"`           // e.g., "opus-4.1"
    TaskDescription string    `json:"task_description"`
    LastActiveTS    time.Time `json:"last_active_ts"`
}

type Message struct {
    ID          int       `json:"id"`
    ThreadID    string    `json:"thread_id"`
    Subject     string    `json:"subject"`
    BodyMD      string    `json:"body_md"`
    From        string    `json:"from"`
    To          []string  `json:"to"`
    Importance  string    `json:"importance"`  // normal, high, urgent
    AckRequired bool      `json:"ack_required"`
    CreatedTS   time.Time `json:"created_ts"`
}

type FileReservation struct {
    ID          int       `json:"id"`
    PathPattern string    `json:"path_pattern"`
    AgentName   string    `json:"agent_name"`
    Exclusive   bool      `json:"exclusive"`
    Reason      string    `json:"reason"`
    ExpiresTS   time.Time `json:"expires_ts"`
}
```

### JSON-RPC 2.0 Protocol
Agent Mail uses JSON-RPC 2.0 over HTTP:
```go
type JSONRPCRequest struct {
    JSONRPC string      `json:"jsonrpc"` // "2.0"
    ID      interface{} `json:"id"`
    Method  string      `json:"method"`  // "tools/call"
    Params  struct {
        Name      string                 `json:"name"`
        Arguments map[string]interface{} `json:"arguments"`
    } `json:"params"`
}

type JSONRPCResponse struct {
    JSONRPC string          `json:"jsonrpc"`
    ID      interface{}     `json:"id"`
    Result  json.RawMessage `json:"result,omitempty"`
    Error   *JSONRPCError   `json:"error,omitempty"`
}
```

## Implementation Notes

- **Connection pooling**: Reuse HTTP connections; Agent Mail may be called frequently
- **Timeout handling**: 5s default for most operations, 30s for search/summarize
- **Retry logic**: Exponential backoff for transient failures (503, timeouts)
- **Bearer token**: Read from NTM config or AGENT_MAIL_TOKEN env var
- **Graceful degradation**: All methods return (result, error); callers handle missing server

 {"id":"ntm-cj0w","title":"Implement seamless agent rotation","description":"## Overview
Execute the full rotation flow: spawn new agent, transfer context, kill old.

## Location
Create `internal/context/rotation.go`

## Rotation Flow
```
1. Detect agent at rotate_threshold
2. Mark agent as "rotating" (no new work)
3. Request handoff summary from agent
4. Wait for summary (with timeout)
5. Spawn new agent with same profile
6. Send handoff summary as initial context
7. Wait for new agent ready
8. Kill old agent pane
9. Update pane metadata
```

## Rotation Coordinator
```go
type Rotator struct {
    monitor  *ContextMonitor
    config   *ContextRotationConfig
    spawner  SpawnInterface
}

func (r *Rotator) CheckAndRotate(sessionName string) error {
    for _, agentID := range r.monitor.AgentsAboveThreshold(r.config.RotateThreshold) {
        if err := r.rotateAgent(sessionName, agentID); err != nil {
            return fmt.Errorf("rotation failed for %s: %w", agentID, err)
        }
    }
    return nil
}

func (r *Rotator) rotateAgent(session, agentID string) error {
    // 1. Get handoff summary
    summary, err := r.getHandoffSummary(session, agentID)
    
    // 2. Spawn replacement with same profile
    newAgentID, err := r.spawnReplacement(session, agentID)
    
    // 3. Initialize new agent with summary
    err = r.sendHandoffContext(session, newAgentID, summary)
    
    // 4. Kill old pane
    err = r.killOldAgent(session, agentID)
    
    // 5. Emit rotation event
    r.emitRotationEvent(agentID, newAgentID)
    
    return nil
}
```

## Edge Cases
- Rotation fails: keep old agent, alert user
- Summary timeout: rotate anyway with best-effort summary
- New agent fails to start: abort rotation
- Multiple agents need rotation: queue and rotate one at a time

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Full rotation flow implemented
- [ ] Graceful handling of failures

 {"id":"ntm-d0z9","title":"Implement staggered prompt delivery","description":"Deliver prompts to agents with configurable delays to prevent thundering herd.

## Core Logic

When spawning N agents with stagger duration D:

```
Agent 1: prompt at T+0
Agent 2: prompt at T+D
Agent 3: prompt at T+2D
...
Agent N: prompt at T+(N-1)*D
```

## Implementation Approaches

### Option A: Timer-Based (Recommended)

Use goroutines with time.AfterFunc:

```go
func (s *Spawner) deliverPromptsStaggered(panes []Pane, prompt string, stagger time.Duration) {
    var wg sync.WaitGroup
    
    for i, pane := range panes {
        delay := time.Duration(i) * stagger
        
        wg.Add(1)
        time.AfterFunc(delay, func() {
            defer wg.Done()
            s.tmux.SendKeys(pane.ID, prompt)
            s.logPromptDelivery(pane, delay)
        })
    }
    
    // Optionally wait for all prompts to be sent
    // wg.Wait()
}
```

Pros: Simple, non-blocking, ntm returns immediately
Cons: If ntm exits, pending prompts not delivered

### Option B: Blocking Delivery

```go
func (s *Spawner) deliverPromptsStaggered(panes []Pane, prompt string, stagger time.Duration) {
    for i, pane := range panes {
        if i > 0 {
            time.Sleep(stagger)
        }
        s.tmux.SendKeys(pane.ID, prompt)
    }
}
```

Pros: Guaranteed delivery, simple
Cons: Blocks for (N-1)*stagger duration

### Option C: Background Process

Spawn a background daemon to handle delayed delivery:
- ntm spawn returns immediately
- Daemon delivers prompts on schedule
- More complex but most robust

## Recommended: Hybrid Approach

1. For interactive use (terminal): Use blocking delivery with progress output
2. For robot mode: Use timer-based, return immediately with schedule
3. Add --wait-stagger flag to optionally block until all prompts sent

## Progress Feedback

During staggered spawn, show progress:

```
Spawning 3 agents with 90s stagger...
  [✓] proj__cc_1 - prompt sent
  [○] proj__cc_2 - prompt in 1m 28s
  [○] proj__cc_3 - prompt in 2m 58s
```

## Edge Cases

1. **ntm exits during stagger**: Log warning, prompts for remaining agents not sent
2. **Agent crashes before prompt**: Skip that agent, continue stagger
3. **User sends manual prompt during wait**: That's fine, our staggered prompt adds to it
4. **Stagger longer than timeout**: Warn user

## Location

internal/robot/spawn.go (new file or extend existing)

## Depends On

- ntm-gnqg: Add --stagger flag to spawn command

- Prompts delivered at correct staggered times
- Progress shown in interactive mode
- Robot mode returns immediately with schedule
- Graceful handling of edge cases

## Rationale
Orchestrating N agents often means broadcasting instructions ("all agents: run tests") or targeted multi-cast ("agents 1,2: work on feature X while agent 3: does Y"). Current approach requires N separate send commands. This consolidates into one atomic operation.

## Usage
```bash
ntm --robot-send myproject --all --msg="run tests and report results"
ntm --robot-send myproject --panes=0.1,0.2 --msg="implement feature X"
ntm --robot-send myproject --type=claude --msg="claude-specific instruction"
```

## Output Structure
```json
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "sent_at": "ISO8601",
  "targets": ["0.1", "0.2", "1.0"],
  "successful": ["0.1", "0.2"],
  "failed": [{"pane": "1.0", "error": "pane not found"}],
  "message_preview": "run tests and..."
}
```

## Features
- --all: broadcast to all panes
- --panes=X,Y,Z: specific pane IDs
- --type=claude: filter by agent type
- --exclude=0.3: exclude specific panes
- --delay-ms=100: stagger sends to avoid thundering herd

## Atomicity
Either report all successes/failures - never partial state. If sending to 5 panes and 2 fail, I need to know which 2 and why.

## Integration with Agent Mail

 {"id":"ntm-kxj7","title":"Feature: CM Server Mode Integration","description":"# CM Server Mode Integration

## The Problem
NTM makes subprocess calls for every CM query:
```go
cmd := exec.Command("cm", "context", task, "--json")
out, err := cmd.Output()  // ~500ms per call
```

This is slow and scales poorly with multiple agents.

## The Solution
CM provides an HTTP MCP server that NTM ignores:
```bash
cm serve --port 8765 --host 127.0.0.1
```

Use the Daemon Supervisor to manage cm serve, then query via HTTP.

## Implementation

### Start via Supervisor
```go
cmSpec := supervisor.DaemonSpec{
    Name:        "cm",
    Command:     "cm",
    Args:        []string{"serve"},
    HealthURL:   "http://127.0.0.1:8765/health",
    PortFlag:    "--port",
    DefaultPort: 8765,
}
daemon, err := supervisor.Start(ctx, cmSpec)
```

### HTTP Client
```go
func (c *CMClient) GetContext(ctx context.Context, task string) (*ContextResult, error) {
    req, _ := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, "POST",
        c.baseURL+"/context",
        strings.NewReader(fmt.Sprintf(`{"task": %q}`, task)),
    )
    // ...
}
```

### Outcome Feedback
```go
func (c *CMClient) RecordOutcome(ctx context.Context, report OutcomeReport) error {
    // POST to /outcome with rule effectiveness data
}
```

## CM Hidden Features (Now Accessible)
- HTTP Server (cm serve)
- Outcome Feedback (cm outcome)
- Session Audit (cm audit)
- Privacy Controls (cm privacy)
- Agent Onboarding (cm onboard)

## New Commands
```bash
ntm memory serve
ntm memory context "implement JWT auth"
ntm memory outcome success --rules b-123,b-456
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CM daemon managed by Supervisor
- [ ] HTTP client for context queries
- [ ] Outcome feedback implemented
- [ ] Latency: ~500ms → <50ms verified
- [ ] Graceful fallback if CM unavailable","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-03T20:17:26.574795-05:00","updated_at":"2026-01-03T20:17:26.574795-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-kxj7","depends_on_id":"ntm-0c15","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-03T20:18:16.854251-05:00","created_by":"daemon","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"ntm-kxj7","depends_on_id":"ntm-bpxu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-03T20:18:17.833192-05:00","created_by":"daemon","metadata":"{}"}]}
